I've been frantically studying Pluto and trying to make sense of what effects his fading out will have, and have recently realized there's a good reason for this as I dive into astrology: Pluto's influence has, quite abruptly (it seems to have taken roughly one Mars cycle) faded out completely in my life. In order to make sense of my life, as reflected in my natal chart, progressions, or transits before early 2019, I need to factor in Pluto as a full planet, and given his placement in my chart, an important one; while now, he's a non-factor. Reviewing my life and astrology, Pluto transits until then were always major, life altering events; while he's currently crossing my ascendant and I don't feel it at all.

I've realized that if this experience, Pluto changing power quite rapidly during the transition to or from planet status, is common, then it explains a few otherwise odd phenomena, including the way that Plutonian phenomena abruptly became very noticeable in 1900, even if limited to a small number of people at first; the way a lot of people find the notion Pluto is fading out incomprehensible and contrary to their experience; the way that so many people are continuing on with the old Plutonian worldview even as many others have completely embraced a fully post-Plutonian world; and the way that right up until the end of the 1920s so many people found the Plutonian elements of society completely baffling. It would also be quite symbolically appropriate, given it creates division in people's lives between when they resonate with Pluto and when it stops are starts, and within society between the people who resonate with Pluto and those who don't.

a) Do you know of anything which could support or refute this theory? My plan is, once I get a much better grasp of astrology, to look into a few dozen notables from 1900-1930, and since 2006, and see if I can find a similar sudden change in other charts; but this will likely be a ways out.

b) If I may throw a question to the readership: Has anyone else had a similar experience to mine?

c) I have an enormous number of bad habits that relate to the remnants of the Plutonian: old ways of thinking and of doing things which no longer make any sense to me but are rooted in inertia and changing only slowly. Do you have any advice on how to speed up the process of clearing these away? I've started LRM and intend to use the Classic Golden Dawn spiritual practice, but at this point in time my skills with it are minimal at best.

replies.title: (no subject)

1) The best way to study it -- the traditional way in astrology -- would be exactly that: to examine as many charts as possible and compare them to what actually happened.

2) My experience was somewhat different. I started studying astrology seriously in 2010, and noticed as soon as I started tracking my transits that I was supposedly dealing with an important aspect to my natal Pluto...and nothing was happening. That was what ultimately got me started on studying the rise and fall of planetary influences! It may well be that whatever Plutonian influence was at work in my life switched off at some point before that, but I've never really resonated well with the Plutonian current.

3) These things take time. Meditation and journaling can be helpful, but replacing one habit with another is always a slow, gradual, non-Plutonian process!

replies.title: The why of the questions - my take
replies.content:
Hi all!
Maybe of interest: I asked - I think three or four of the kids-questions and have read the other Q&A on this theme with avid interest, because I'm just starting out and have a little one. For me the "Problem" was twofold: one is: not knowing enough about the pilosohical and practical ramification of magic; JMG's distinction between e.g. natural magic and ceremonial magic was quite important for me and two: not going back to the FAQ after having read questions here but thinking further on the questions asked and what the answer would be meaning for my Situation. I wonder if JMG could use a standard open source programming answer to beginners questions: Read the source, Luke

replies.title: Re: The why of the questions - my take
replies.content:
No doubt! The fascinating thing for me is that nearly all other beginner's questions can be settled easily: if they're in the FAQ, people go there, read them, and say, "Oh, I get it!" and go away satisfied, and if not, I give them an answer and the same thing happens. In this one case, people get weird.

I'll cite the most common example: the definition of "basic magical practices." The FAQ says "basic magical practices such as the Sphere of Protection, the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram, or the Middle Pillar exercise are fine." That is to say, these three rituals are fine. I've talked about them over and over again as basic magical practices -- the things that an occultist in training does every day. And yet I constantly have people trying to rule lawyer some kind of ornate redefinition of "basic magical practices" to include something other than those simple daily magical rituals. That;s why I finally yelled at a commenter who engaged in an unusually baroque version of said rule lawyering and shut the door on more questions on this subject.

I quite understand not knowing the difference between ceremonial magic and natural magic if you're just starting out, and in fact it occurs to me that inserting a discussion of that in the Magic Monday FAQ might be a good thing. But sending people back to the FAQ is what I've tried to do.

replies.title: Re: The why of the questions - my take
replies.content:
Maybe it’s because you wrote "such as"? That could make some people think that there are more basic practices out there "such as" the three you explicitely stated.

Maybe it would help to change the sentence into "basic magical practices, i.e. the SOP, Middle Pillar exercise, and Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram".

replies.title: Re: The why of the questions - my take

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
(b) Quite simple. The person reading the instructions doesn't like what the instructions have to say in (and it's in crystal-clear language!), thus their mental hamster wheel starts turning as they fish for alternative interpretations and semantic loopholes so they can then justify to themselves that in fact they can have small children of their own around the house AND do complex ceremonial magic after all!!

I think this plays into a larger cultural issue that's so prevalent today. Many of us modern/postmodern Westerners don't like the idea of making lifestyle sacrifices, especially in light of the gazillion-and-one distractions, diversions, and glitzy games that's shoved in our faces 24/7/365; and especially for the sake of raising children in a sound, sane, and wholesome manner. This is a big reason why the fertility rate has sunk far below the replacement level for the West's comfortable classes. With today economic arrangements and self-centered culture of high-tech diversions, kids simply get in the way of the hedonistic lifestyle that's so readily available to us who wish to extend adolescence well into middle age and beyond.

So we can see how this mentality might carry over into the occult sphere.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I think that's involved in some cases, but not in all, because I've seen the same sort of mental block in other situations. Many years ago, for example, I knew a guy who belonged to the same lodge I did, and he could not get his head around the fact that it met on the second and fourth Thursdays of every month. We'd point out that you look at the calendar page, find the column of Thursdays, count down to the second and fourth, and mark that, but he'd go, "Yes, but..." and get all tangled up trying to make sense of it in some other way. There was a definite mental block involved. We started printing out a calendar page for him with those two dates circled, and he was fine, but he could not make sense of it himself.

So I suspect for some people, giving them a list of basic magical practices they can do sets off the same reaction. They can't just use the list. They have to try to figure out some baroque underlying logic behind it, and that trips them up (and annoys the bejesus out of those who try to say, "No, just READ WHAT IT SAYS!").

Again, doubtless you're right that there are people who fuss about such things because they can't tolerate the idea that having kids limits what they can do -- we've all seen that often enough. But it's not the only factor.

I've added two little ones to my family in the last few years, and it's really been a struggle to set my other plans on hold and and just accept that I'm not getting anything else done for a few years. (There have been a few other things going on in the world lately that have also caused people to set plans on hold, too, of course...). So you get people who are very used to setting goals and fulfilling them (reading a lot into focused will, etc., too), who suddenly can't get anything done, because they're sleep-deprived and chasing kids around, and in many cases one of their go-to responses to improving their lives and getting stuff changed is Doing a Ritual, so they really want to do that and now they can't. And there are a few very ambitious mages on this forum (the type of person who is asking you for a 30-year curriculum, etc.) and so they don't want to get behind, but they can't stand the thought of just not doing this for a few years.

For me, personally, I've been thankful for the firm guidance - I have enough other things to focus on that I appreciate the "permission" to not worry about this for a while!

Good evening!

To anyone who celebrates it, a very happy Easter! Otherwise, a very pleasant Sunday. I've been rather free with asking questions here, so I'd like to start offering something of value as well. Tonight, please consider checking out Eirik Westcoat's Viking Poetry for Heathen Rites, even if you're not a heathen yourself. Almost anything, as long as it isn't by Scott Cunningham!

comments.title: Factors in Reincarnation
comments.content:
I found your post on reincarnation on your other blog interesting ("A Few Notes on Reincarnation"). If possible, could you describe what sort of factors guide a soul to incarnate as a particular kind of person in terms of character? Does a soul choose or seek a future incarnation on the basis of an issue or need it's trying to resolve in its development? Is there some sort of hierarchy or classification of needs/issues for souls that they work through? (Kind of parallel to the way children develop, progressing through developmental periods for movement, language, mathematics, morality, socialization etc. in a semi-predictable fashion).

I ask because I have grown up all my life around drug addicts and people with various other afflictions and sometimes they resolve their issues before die, and sometimes they don't, and the process fascinates me. The ways in which it parallels child development also have given me a lot of food for thought, as I work with children.

As a secondary question you don't have to answer, do certain souls get drawn to certain cultures on the basis of a developmental imperative?

I understand if reincarnation is not currently not understood this completely.

replies.title: Re: Factors in Reincarnation
replies.content: The processes that guide reincarnation are subtle, complex, and intensely personal. Each incarnation is a reflection of whatever collection of karma the soul most needs to work with, and whatever aspect of the soul's ultimate destiny it is ready to work on at that time. It can't be boiled down to anything so simplistic as a developmental hierarchy, since each soul's karma and destiny are unique. It's also not something the soul chooses, not until very late in the process of human incarnation; for most souls, it's as reflexive as birth pangs.

comments.content:
JMG,

1) I practice discursive meditation, but the connections I make seem rather trivial and obvious. Is there any value in drawing ‘obvious’ connections?

2) Is it possible to meditate on a single topic indefinitely? After re-treading the same ground over and over again, at one point does one exhaust its potential?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The obvious points are worth starting with, but always try to push beyond them. "What else might this imply?" is a good question always to ask yourself.

2) It's not helpful. Go on to something else, and then consider returning to the old topic when you think you can come up with something new concerning it.

3) Prayer is massively misunderstood. The gods are not vending machines, and prayer is not a way of getting things from them. Prayer is making contact. It's entering into a relationship and then letting the relationship ripen.

4) The gods already know what you need, and the difference between that and what you think you need. Again, they're not vending machines, and the surest way to spoil any relationship is to treat it purely as a way of getting stuff you want.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
On 3 and 4: I've been reading "The Life of Apollonius of Tyana" and he recommended fairly short prayers along the lines of "thy will be done." Or that is how I read things like "[g]ive me, ye Gods, what is my due" and "[g]rant me, ye Gods, to have little and need naught." Is this kind of short prayer, acknowledging the gods, maybe including some appropriate praise, and a desire to accept their will, a good approach to praying?

I know folks who go into lots of detail in their prayers, carefully laying out what is bothering them and what they want their god(s) to do about it. That always seemed unnecessary to me. And I've noticed they seldom get the thing they're asking for. Honestly, I've never been able to engage in that type of prayer. When invoking a saint, for example, I almost always say something like "St. Joan of Arc, pray for me and my family."

My prayers to the Anglo-Saxon gods have followed a similar format: "Hail to Thunor, protector of mankind. I am Brenainn Griffudd, son of Roy. I seek to know you and to accept your will in my life. Please accept this offering of beer."

replies.title: (no subject)

If that last doesn’t get you on Thunor’s good side, I don’t know what would! 😄

—Princess Cutekitten

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It really depends on the person. Apollonius' prayers weren't just "Thy will be done" -- he was asking for two very simple but profound things: first, to get exactly what his actions in life deserved, and second, to reduce his dependence on material things to the absolute minimum. Since he was a classical mystic, those were major goals -- perfect justice and perfect independence -- and he may have phrased them tersely but he was asking for a couple of very, very important gifts.

I know people who prefer long prayers, and get results, though they're not doing the "gimme this, gimme that" kind of prayer -- it's more a long, affectionate conversation with a much older and wiser friend. On the other hand, Her Kittenship is right -- your prayer to Thunor strikes me as about as perfect as such a prayer could be.

It supports email with attachments, position reporting, weather and information bulletins, and is well-known for its role in interoperable emergency and disaster relief communications. It is capable of operating completely without the internet--automatically--using smart-network radio relays. comments.title: Gods and Archangels
comments.content:
I am drawn to the Hellenic or Hellenistic worldview rather than to the Abrahamic. However lately I've found myself thinking a lot about archangels. It began when I started invoking the Violet Flame. In that practice, one is advised first to request protection from Archangel Michael, and to thank him for same afterward. Now the ideas and images of Gabriel and Raphael have also become prominent in my mind. In fact, I have taken to sometimes praying to them for the respective blessings for which they are known.

You can ask a Hellenic deity for assistance, and maybe you will get it, or maybe you will get something else. Ask an angel, and they're there to help; even if you don't get it, they're not out to get you. Whereas if a Hellenic god is annoyed with you, he will happily drop kick you and stomp you with hobnail boots until you clean up your act. So far I haven't had that experience with angels.

I'm a visually oriented person. I love beauty, and according to the classic artists angels are beautiful beings of light, so I find the thought of them agreeable to dwell upon in my mind's eye.

Am I a hopeless spiritual slut? Am I on two irreconcilable paths that will split me in half, or is there a way philosophically to resolve these spiritual spheres?

replies.title: Re: Gods and Archangels
replies.content:
One of the odder spiritual phenomena of the last century or so is that the archangels and angels seem to be detaching themselves from the Abrahamic tradition. That's not a new thing entirely -- the late pagan Neoplatonists made room for angels and archangels in their vision of the cosmos -- but it's become especially striking over the last half century or so. Ross Nichols used to invoke the four archangels in his Druid ceremonies, for example, with very good results.

A great many people seem to be finding their way to entirely new constellations of spiritual experience and practice just now. My suggestion is that instead of spiritual slut-shaming yourself, so to speak, you accept that your path leads in strange directions. You might consider invoking Raphael and asking him for guidance in this matter, as that's one of the things he's noted for. Here he is in a painting, guiding Tobit...
[IMAGE:0]

replies.title: Re: Gods and Archangels
replies.content:
So, we get a chance to talk about St. Uriel, how joyful!

TL;DR. The Catholic Church wont let you pray to archangel Uriel. Why not, because in 2002 Pope John Paul II did uphold a ban from the 8th century where the cult to angels not explicitly named in the (Canonical) books of the Bible. From a strictly legalistic point of view, Papal Infallibility means each Pope has the prerogative to dictate faith an morals for the Church, end of story. But, if you analyze what was actually said at the Council of Rome (A.D. 745) and why, you may reach the conclusion that the prohibition was an overreach meant to correct one specific case of heresy: one self proclaimed bishop named Aldebert. To be fair, the name of Uriel is explicitly condemned (actually said to be a demon) alongside a prayer composed by this Aldebert, but you could interpret this as either 1) All names of angels (but St. Michael's) in this prayer are actually names of demons, or 2) The beings responding to the prayer of this heretic may actually be demons, because we cannot be sure the names are actually of angels, because those names are extra-Biblical.

If I have caught you interest, you probably should read the whole article and make up your own mind.

replies.title: Re: Gods and Archangels

It’s already surrounded by quite a bit of solemnity.

I can’t say I’ve devoted more than 5 minutes of thought to the archangel issue, but technically, it appears you can pray to Uriel, you just have to address him as “Dear sir” rather than by name. As in the Litany of the Saints, where he’s covered under “All you holy angels and archangels.” Me, I figure if the Orthodox, who are generally pretty no-nonsense about matters religious, feel it’s okay to invoke all the archangels, it probably is. Although the only archangel I ever bother is St. Michael, and he’s got a big sword 🗡 and stomped Satan‘s [unDruidly word], so nobody, not even the Pope, messes with him. So if I end up doing another million years in Purgatory, I guess it won’t be for a name violation.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
From personal experience, and your mileage, will, of course vary:

On Sources:

Wotan/Woden/Odin/*Wodhanaz seems to be a very complex God/set of Gods, so any overly shallow renditions will likely leave you cold if that's not what you're looking for.

From a scholarly standpoint, one book I have found very helpful is Odin: The One-Eyed God and the (Indo-) Germanic Mannerbund" by Kris Kershaw. It draws on a lot of cross-cultural lore about figures in various Indo-European-derived societies that seem to have had things in common with the better-attested Germanic one-eyed trickster/magician/wanderer/king/sacrifice God.

If you'd like to go more mythological, all of the core Germanic sources will feature lots of Wotan - the Eddas, the Yngling Saga, and so forth. If you're looking for a good guide on "where to look", Simek's Encyclopedia of Northern Mythology is extermely handy for pointing you in useful directions, as is Grimm's Teutonic Mythology (you can find online copies on Google Books, starting here: https://www.google.com/books/edition/Teutonic_Mythology/neQtAAAAIAAJ?hl=en&gbpv=0 or in print through the Cambridge University Press version).

On offerings:
I have primarily given drink and incense offerings. For drinks, whiskey (high-proof bourbon or rye) has seemed to go over well for me, though Mead or Wine would be traditional. Coffee also seems fitting to me, for a God who loves both knowledge and altered states. Horses and men seem pretty impractical these days, for a variety of reasons.

For incense, I tend to go with Frankincense & Myrhh, as these are "kingly", but there's also a whole set of Northern/Germanic herbal practice to consider, such as that laid down in Leechcraft by Stephen Pollington (Anglo-Saxon healing texts and interpretation) and The Northern Shamanic Herbal by Raven Kaldera (meant to be more immediately practical, and based on more personal Gnosis, but a guide to herbcraft from the standpoint of a spirit worker with a relationship with Woden).

Hope some of the above helps!
Jeff

comments.title: Whence the Power of Symbols
comments.content:
I would like to hear your take on the magical power of symbols (and from anyone else who cares to chime in).

In a non-magical sense, symbolic communication (such as described in Semiotics and the Philosophy of Language) involves 1) a symbol itself, 2) the meaning given to it by the speaker, and 3) the meaning given to it by the listener. Communication works best when both of those meanings match pretty closely. Occult theory, if I understand correctly, generally sees symbols as having a sort of life of their own, an existence on the astral plane, which we could add to those above as a fourth factor.

My question is, what gives magical symbols their power? Let's take the swastika, which was a common symbol in Asia long before the nazis got ahold of it. Does the meaning given to it by the nazis have any bearing on its use where it traditionally had other meanings? It is still used to mark the location of Buddhist temples on maps in Japan, for example, and German nationalism is pretty far down the list of associations for people here.

I decided it was time to give David Icke a fair hearing, and I am half-way through one of his books just now. One of his assertions is that black cubes, the star of David, and many other symbols are covert symbols of Saturn and Satanism and such. Whether that is true or not, I wonder whether that kind of covert symbol could have an evil effect on people who are unaware of the evil meaning intended by it. Going back to semiotics, if I use a word to mean A, but you understand it to mean B, we're going to be confused. If I use a hexagram as a satanic symbol and put it where you can see it, but you understand it to mean something completely different, well then what?

replies.title: Re: Whence the Power of Symbols
replies.content: There's swastikas repeated in the facade of an insurance building in Philadelphia built in the 1920's. There is a sign explaining it was a symbol of prosperity at that time.

replies.title: Re: Whence the Power of Symbols
replies.content:
Just a member of the peanut gallery here, but hold on... symbols being used simultaneously to represent both Saturn and Satan? Call me skeptical. With Saturn representing such things as self-discipline and limitations, he seems like pretty much the worst possible fit for Satanism. That'd be like using the biohazard symbol to indicate when an instrument or room has been sterilized for surgery.

I realize you're just citing Icke, but if that's what he's saying it seems like his thinking is a little off-track.

(a rather saturnine) Dijon Exogenous Muffin

replies.title: Re: Whence the Power of Symbols
replies.content: Icke's thinking isn't just a little off track. He's nucking futs, as the saying goes.

replies.title: Re: Whence the Power of Symbols
replies.content: I'm not going to defend David Icke, but I'm still curious if what he describes is even possible. When the nazis hijacked the swastika, did they give it some meaning and power that it didn't have before? If so, does that have an effect on swastikas already extant? Could the same thing be done covertly - i.e. take some popular symbol of peace and prosperity and imbue it with a nefarious power unknown to the people who are using it?

replies.title: Re: Whence the Power of Symbols
replies.content: Only to a very limited extent, and for a short time. You can, using rituals, charge a symbol with an intention -- that's what the Nazis were doing at the Nuremberg rallies, which were large-scale magical rituals. If the meaning of the symbol and the intention you try to put into it are in conflict, however, it's going to come unraveled pretty quickly.

replies.title: Re: Whence the Power of Symbols
replies.content:
Semiotics is the study of signs, not symbois. A sign has a meaning that can be entirely defined by the meanings given it by the participants in a communicative act. A symbol's meaning cannot be so defined, because it is not arbitrary, as the meanings of a sign are. To make sense of the power of symbols it's necessary to accept the existence of a continuum of meanings not limited to the inside of any one person's head.

What's happened over the last few centuries can be described fairly well as the attempted dumbing down of symbols into signs. The swastika's a great example. It's a variation on the sun-wheel, an old symbol of life force, which is why the Nazis hijacked it. It now serves as a sign of Nazism -- or more precisely of anything someone wants to associate with Nazism. In Japan, where the original meaning is still present, it's still used in an older sense. I suspect, in fact, that one of the reasons Nazism is still as potent in the collective imagination as it is, is precisely that the deeper meanings of the swastika filter through the sign.

Back in ancient times, before refrigeration, the only way to keep meat fresh was to keep it on the hoof. In those days, whenever an animal was killed for meat, prayers were said over it and certain parts were offered up to one or more deities. That was the old context for animal sacrifice. Hello,

This is how my partner prepares her sage and uses it:

Roll the leaves together into a small bundle,with a few stems sort of sticking out at one end so as to obtain a kind of 'handle', our bundles are usually about one to one and half inch in diameter (+-2.5 to 4 cm.). Tie the bundle up with natural string (to avoid toxic fumes). The sage stick or bundle can be as tight as you wish, but usually we will tighten just enough so that the packet does not break up easily. Sometimes we will incorporate other plants to the bundle so as to obtain a different scent and cleansing effect. The bundles available to purchase are usually wrapped up in a very compact manner, you can if you prefer do the same.

The stick or bundle can be used immediately or left to dry somewhat, this being a question of personal preference that you will be able to establish after experimenting a few times.

To use, light up the bundle and either hand carry it and envelop the object or person with the smoke while praying for the sage to cleanse and remove the negative energies and entities. BE very careful not to create a fire hazard, loose bundles burn quite fast and might produce an open flame...act accordingly !

You can also light up the bundle and place it in a small saucer or plate or any other non combustible shallow recipient, and while praying simply let the bundle burn slowly while filling up the air of the room that is being smudged (slightly open a window so that the smoke can carry away the negative energies). Let it extinguish on it's own. As another poster wrote this is akin to burning incense.

Slightly open up the windows and doors so that the smoke can carry outside of the building the negative energies, but make sure that the smoke can also totally 'fumigate' the walls and objects in the room. Pray or recite intentions while cleansing with the sage bundle. For the final smudging of the body he suggested obtaining someone's help. If the bundle is still burning afterwards, simply let it extinguish itself on it's own in a fireproof recipient. If the weather is to cold to cleanses one's body outside simply do so in the homes entrance (door partially open). For a thorough cleansing, he recommended that one should also smudge every piece of furniture and major object present in every room while doing the walk-through. His mother would thoroughly cleanse their house every new season, when a particularly bad event happened or when things started to get nasty and negative.

Hope this proves helpful.

replies.title: Re: Question about novena prayers
replies.content:
First of all, please accept my condolences for the difficulties you and your wife have had, and my very best wishes for a successful pregnancy.

1) Spells and prayers are actually more effective if you don't speak the language. That's why so many traditional churches keep (or used to keep) their prayers and rituals in older languages, such as Latin or Church Slavonic, and why magical practitioners use words of power in ancient languages. Just concentrate on saying the prayer as clearly as possible, concentrating on your intention, and you'll do well.

2) Both of these are good.

3) There ought to be a saint in your part of Europe who has a reputation for helping childless couples have children. I'm guessing from your first name that you're either Croatian or Slovenian; a quick check online finds Saint Vincenca, the patron of Blato, who is invoked for childbirth, and the Blessed Martyrs of Drina, who seem to be invoked for just about everything. Doubtless there are others. Choose one or more of them, pray to them in the standard Catholic way, and ask their intercession; that's a classic way to seek help, and by all accounts it's an effective one.

replies.title: (no subject)

You're dealing with the god, not the astrological energy! That's always a good way to deal with afflicted planets -- the reason why planetary charity works so well with afflicted planets is that the offering calls on the divine consciousness rather than its astrological expression. I did a divination asking the I Ching about the results of the Ukraine war, and if it's ok, I'd like to share my reading here.

For Ukraine, the results are 15.6: Modesty, into 52: The Mountain/Keeping Still.
For Russia, the results are 51.2.5: Thunder/The Arousing, into 58: The Lake/The Joyous
(I mostly base hexagram names and interpretations on the Wilhelm translation)

The Ukrainian people could for all I know be the most humble people in the world, but Ukraine's government has been the exact opposite of modest, and so I take I interpret #15 as saying that they will be humbled through defeat.
Line 6, which speaks of sending armies marching into your own city/country to chastise your own people, seems to indicate that they will also experience some sort of civil conflict, possibly involving the government and the various militias that are only nominally under its command. The line implies that this is a positive or necessary, action, so hopefully it will have a good result.
And in regards to #52, I take it to mean that Ukraine will experience a period of stillness following the current period of frantic activity, when it can take the time to soberly reflect on the preceding actions and events. On another level, The Mountain might also mean that Ukraine will become a landlocked nation after Russia takes all its coastline leaving a rump state in the west as some have suggested they will, with western Ukraine also being the only part of the country with any mountains to speak of.

For Russia, Thunder, symbolizing a long-building energy which surges out suddenly and even violently when it finally finds release, seems to me a perfect image of this war and I thus take it as a positive symbol in this martial context.
The changing lines both speak of danger and repeated shocks, as well as of something being lost and later coming back in line 2 and nothing being lost in line 5. At first I thought that line 2 might refer to Russia regaining the land that had been given to Ukraine by the Soviet leaders, but it and line 5 might instead refer to Russia's economy, which will doubtless suffer some shocks in the near term but which the Russians expect to sort out before the year is over.
On the other hand, all of this might instead be saying that the end of the war in Ukraine will not be the end of war for Russia, and that the country will find itself involved in some other conflict following the end of the Ukraine war.
As for The Joyous/Lake, it suggests to me firstly that the Russian people will be happy with the outcome. On a geographic level that parallels that of #51 in the Ukraine reading, it could also be heralding Russia's control over the north coast of the Black Sea after taking that coastline from Ukraine. And, lastly, it might be pointing to Russia enjoying a close friendship with other states; now that Russian society has largely given up on reconciling with the west, they can focus their efforts on building relationships with other countries which promise to be more fruitful than the one with modern Europe could ever be.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I'm looking for any advice on getting out of a firmly entrenched holding pattern when it comes to...well, everything, but for the purposes of this blog, occult studies and religious practice. I've been stuck on the first step for many years, I believe thanks to burn out, total lack of confidence and a certainty that nothing I do will work out and why keep beating my head against that brick wall that lives two feet away from my start line.

I've spent plenty of time thinking it over (that's one thing I'm good at), so I know what is at the root of a lot of it. I was raised, by parents, teachers and classmates alike, to believe I was at best an incompetent failure who can never do anything right, and at worst a dangerous monster whose actions were just going to hurt somebody. Life consisted of a lot of heavily scripted plays where I was forced to act these end results out so I could learn my place (thankfully the ritual plays were focused mostly on the incompetent loser angle while the dangerous monster one was left mostly to words; so now it's the former that plays out in my life, the latter remains a worry deep in the recesses of my mind but doesn't actually happen). The only break I got, from those rare authority figures that both didn't despise me and recognized how I was being treated, was the It Gets Better narrative, or a version of it. One where everyone is entitled to a happy ending and life just rearranges itself when you turn eighteen and leave your hometown; with a change of scenery everything will just come together, even if that is never the way you've known it, because reasons! No doubt that is true for many people, but I was not one of them; that narrative also doesn't acknowledge that limitations exist and gives you no way to cope with it's failure.

I know I made a lot of mistakes, and I wish I had taken that burst of positive energy that propelled me out of my mother's house and into the world and spent it very differently than I did (I would've turned inward and spent it building myself up, within the limitations I have, let the things outside of myself go). That's not what I did though. That positive energy is long gone, I've built a mountain of failure, built the habit of failure. Now, though I want to get out of it, it's hard to, hard to think that it's anything but a pointless waste of effort.

The religion angle is the worst part of it. I had a very powerful experience with a god when I was younger, which enabled and fueled that aforementioned positive energy. It was the sort of thing I once looked at as a calling, I was prepared to treat it as one (though it was a Pagan God rather than the Christian one, which meant it took much longer, and the wading through of much garbage, before I found anything that told me how I should handle that). That relationship is gone now. There was such a great length of time between the initial event and when I had no choice but to acknowledge that the door there had closed, that I can't know for sure what happened. There are various narratives I tell myself about it, they all make as much sense of events as I remember them. Right now, I lean toward thinking it was a one time thing (contact with this god), meant to accomplish one thing, that I and the Pagans then around me all misinterpreted it, made it more than it was. That one encounter, and the probably misguided personal mythology I built up around it, got me through a lot and kept going for much longer than it reasonably should have since I doubt there was anything solid behind it for years; the loss of it, the contracting of that personal mythology to it was just this one thing over and done with, I think it might have been more devastating than the real life failure. It was where I placed my hope, and it's gone.

In spite of how it went, the idea of a religiously focused life still appeals. I try to move on, but that is another holding pattern. Part of it is I don't know what to do; can't follow my interests, already did that and it lead to a dead end. The polytheist world is a bit to big to wander through with no direction. But I know that, under that lack of interest in any of the particular options before me, is a deep seated mistrust, both of my own instincts that steered me wrong before, and of the gods in general. There is a little voice in the back of my mind that keeps whispering nothing out there wants to hear from you; if anything does respond, best not to get too invested, it'll be gone before you know it. I don't like that I think this way, but it is the experience that I had (or all that I can understand of it).

I don't really want to be stuck here forever, but I also don't know how to get passed all these massive hurdles. Too much advice out there acts like the things that happened in your life can either be ignored or that they don't matter, but my mind won't play along with that. I imagine with my lack of magical skill, some sort of affirmation is my best bet, but I admit I have no idea how to speak positively to myself (the few times I've gotten around the failure block, it's been by berating myself into action). Some place to start would be helpful.

I know the matter of dealing with the gods is separate from the moving forward with occult studies, the latter is theoretically easier because it involves only myself. If anyone at all has advice on moving forward there, I would appreciate hearing it. (apologies for the giant wall of text)

Start by accepting where you are. Pushing against the blockage in the familiar ways is just holding the blockage in place.

Second, consider doing some of the very gentle will exercises listed here.

Third, and most difficult, get a notebook and start writing down everything you like about being a failure. Censor nothing. Just let yourself write. Once you figure out what you gain from the existing pattern -- and such patterns do not stay in place unless there's some reward somewhere in the game -- you can start doing more journaling, to unpack the various emotional factors that hold the existing mess in place.

The loss of that divine relationship is a greater shadow over everything. I can pin point that realization as the moment when things got a lot worse. I don't know what I'm supposed to do about, since I can't know what happened and there is little I can do to change the outcome.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Dear Anonymous -

Regarding failure and occult study (and otherwise), the journaling idea JMG suggested is sound. A way to compliment this is to take up a purification and blessing practice while exploring (not fighting) the blockage in a respectful and gentle manner. Cold water washing of the face, mouth and arms, and misting of the face with rose water afterwards is one example (flip a coin for yes/no regarding the fortunateness of this in your specific case if you have no other divination training).

Regarding the religious experience aspect, I have more to say -

If I have heard and understood correctly, it sounds like you feel that your relationship with the pagan deity you referenced is over.

Perhaps it is, perhaps it isn’t - I can’t comment to that. Sometimes it happens that a relationship is short term and for one purpose, and when it’s over, it’s over.

What I would like to share, however, is an observation about the intensity of religious experience. Very few of them are as ecstatic and enervating as what you mentioned. And once the energy of that processes out, a kind of depression can result, where all is felt is absence. Like before all was Light and Possibility, and now there is but only the Emptiness of Outer Darkness and Alone.

And from there psychologizing can take over, and the assumption that one has been left and rejected seems the most logical. It makes personal what is a function of human biology and psychology - what can happen when there is a huge energetic high (particularly when the energy bodies need some TLC), and then that energy flows onward to where it is meant to go. Because - and this is my personal belief - the blessing of the divine is not only for you. It flows through you (the “metaphorical you”), blesses you personally, and then it flows into your sphere and wherever your sphere intersects, and then onward.

Blessing that stagnates is blockage.

From my perspective, it sounds like you had a very successful religious experience with that divinity. The comedown from the “connective moment” can be brutal, however, particularly the first time.

From here, where will you go? It’s up to you. If you want to pray to that deity and see if there is an open door, it is your choice. There are many other options available to you. For instance, you could pray to your natal guardian angel for blessing and guidance toward a beneficial divine relationship.

Best wishes on your journey, and good luck. And thanks for reading my giant wall of text.

Amaranth Discombobulated Durian

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
One tactic you might find helpful on the practical side of things is to focus on consistency and small wins, rather than overall outcomes. A lot of the self-help literature focuses on setting goals, because many people find these very motivating. But sometimes goals are intimidating and it's helpful to be like "okay, I'll figure out exactly where I'm going later, but right now, I need to just do something."

Also, when I say "small" wins, I mean really small. Embarrassingly small. JMG's will exercises he linked show this tactic in the early steps ("Touch your nose ten times").

The same approach can help with more practical stuff as well. If you're feeling down and having trouble getting out of bed, getting to the couch is a win. If you're trying to eat less sugar, having a smaller piece of cake is a win. Trying to get active? Doing a single push-up is a win. Working on your occult practice? A single round of the fourfold breath is a win.

Ideally, these little wins snowball, and some slightly bigger stuff starts feeling doable before too long, but a lot of us won't take the first steps because we feel like they're "not good enough" or "I'll never get there".

Good luck!
Jeff

replies.title: Re: These Questions Three
replies.content:
1) Good heavens, yes. It happens all the time.

replies.title: Re: Anger
replies.content:
The Stoics have a lot of helpful advice regarding anger, specifically learning to notice the distinction between the stimulus that prompts your anger, the angry emotional reaction, and any thoughts that contribute to that reaction. I try to make a habit of catching my initial reaction and reinterpreting it more accurately. So, for example, while driving, I might think "that pig tickling cabbage face just cut me off!" Then I pause, take a breath, and remind myself "okay, that guy just did something that I found unexpected and inconvenient that also triggered my fear of getting hurt."

When it comes to marriage, very often anger will come up due to communication issues (shocking revelation, I know!). One very helpful habit is to learn to avoid universalizing/totalizing statements ("you always do ____" or "you never do ______") and to try to focus on external, tangible things, rather than internal states/motivations (so, "you are turned away from me and it looks like you're frowning, so I'm wondering how you're feeling" rather than "why are you mad at me?").

Lastly, on prayer, if you are open to polytheism, I would just point out that different Gods might have different ways of helping. I pray to Germanic Gods, and Thunor seems to be very helpful in reminding me to live up to my responsibilities and be okay with doing what I need to do for my wife and family, whereas Frigg helps me see my wife's side of things and the value in a harmonious household in its own sake.

Hope these help, and good luck!
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Anger
replies.content:
First of all, have you considered counseling from a competent professional? This is the sort of thing they do for a living, you know.

Second, since I don't know you and can't look into your soul, I can't tell you who or what to pray to. That's got to be your call.

One thing that may be useful, however, is a teaching I got from the late John Gilbert. He always used to say that anger is a secondary emotion -- we learn the habit of getting angry because it's easier to feel anger than to feel some other emotion, such as shame, grief, or fear. Dig down to the primary emotion and you uncover the root of your anger. That's the only way it's ossible to heal.

comments.title: Experience during meditation
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I was hoping that you could help me to make sense of a strange experience I had while meditating a few years ago, before I began studying western occultism.

At the time, I was a practicing Buddhist, of the westernized zen variety. I was attending the weekly service and my teacher was reading aloud to the class the Hsin Hsin Ming. (A fabulous and poetic short zen text).

On that day, my zazen practice was quite refined. I let all my thoughts come and go freely, clinging to them only minimally. My teacher’s voice poured like a gentle river into my awareness, and my mind was stiller than it had ever been - before or since. “If you wish to move in the one Way, do not dislike even the world of senses and ideas,”she intoned. “Indeed, to accept them fully is identical with true enlightenment. The wise man strives to no goals, but the foolish man fetters himself.”

Suddenly, without my bidding, my breathing became rapid. My heart raced. My consciousness altered significantly from my ordinary waking consciousness and even from my typical meditation consciousness - to a degree I had only ever experienced before on psychedelics (although the quality of the experience was nothing like a psychedelic).

I felt my body humming with what felt like electricity. It was like I was filled with lightning. Every fibre thrummed with energy. It’s hard to describe, but I was in a radically different sort of relationship to my own body. It was exhilarating, ecstatic, and almost too much to bear. Despite the overwhelming sensations, I kept to my meditation practice and stayed with my breath. For several minutes I remained in this state, almost gasping for air, before it was too much for me and I had to open my eyes. The sensation subsided.

Afterwards, I was completely shook. I tried to talk to my teacher about it, but her response was something like, “yeah, that kind of thing sometimes happens. Best not to talk about it.”

This was terribly disappointing to me at the time. Something intense had happened to me, which seemed to point to a kind of transcendent possibility… and I was told basically to pretend it hadn’t happened. There may have been some lesson in my teacher’s response, but it was surely lost on me. That ended up being the beginning of the end of my practice of Buddhism. I kept at it for about two more years, but never as seriously as I had before.

What happened to me that day was a red flashing light that materialism - even Buddhist materialism of the sort so many westerners practice - didn’t tell the whole story. I read some Buddhist texts on such topics as the jhanas to try to make sense of the experience, but they didn’t quite work - or perhaps I just didn’t grasp the explanations.

Do you have any insight into what occurred during that experience? I guess that it was something to do with getting in touch with my etheric body… but I really don’t know. Any ideas would would be greatly appreciated, as this has been a big mystery for me.

Thank you for your help!

replies.title: Re: Experience during meditation
replies.content: That was a standard low-intensity kundalini awakening. If you read accounts of kundalini experiences you'll find many close parallels, though every such experience is a little different. What happens when the kundalini stirs is that it flows through the subtle channels in the body; a full release right up the spine can literally kill you, unless you're under the supervision of a capable guru and have done lots of preliminary work, but the kind of thing you experienced mostly just shifts your spirituality to a higher gear by running additional energy through your subtle body. If you continue to practice meditation, so long as you set aside any attempt to grasp at a similar experience, you're likely to have more such bursts, each of which will raise your "vibes" further and help your subtle body get used to working at that higher intensity of energy.

replies.title: Re: Experience during meditation
replies.content: Thank you very much for the guidance.

comments.title: Cawl and Gyro
comments.content:
Any idea why William Price's ritual union suit had Greek letters all over it? Is there some reason you know of, apart from his own personal eccentricity, why revival druids of his day would have thought that the Greek alphabet and Greek lore in general was nifty?

The reason I'm asking is because, having come from a sola scriptura Christian background, I've made a personal connection with the Greek letters and the Koine Greek language through studying the New Testament in the original. Wondering about their occult significance led me to Nigel Pennick's wonderful book Magical Alphabets. Since then I've started practicing the CGD, in which you mention that revival druid legend tells of Pythagoras having come to Prydain and shared knowledge with the druids. Once I've spent a good year or five with the CGD (yes, just as it's written LOL) I think I'm going to want to explore how the Greek alphabet, along with certain other bits of Greekiness like the tetractys, might fit in to the system. So I'm curious if there are any other connections between revival druidry and Greek spirituality, philosophy, or esotericism that could form a fun mythic basis for combining them.

replies.content: That would take serious experimentation to work out. I suspect, for what it's worth, that the Eye of Horus can't be used to invoke -- it's a protective symbol pure and simple, and you'd need to find a different Egyptian symbol to invoke.
